name: Aitareya Upanishad
summary: The Aitareya Upanishad from the Rigveda describes the creation of the universe from the Self, the creation of the human being, and ultimately declares that consciousness (prajna) is Brahman — prajnanam brahma.

verses.sanskrit:
आत्मा वा इदमेक एवाग्र आसीत् | नान्यत् किञ्चन मिषत् |
स ईक्षत लोकान्नु सृजा इति ||१.१.१||
verses.transliteration:
ātmā vā idam eka evāgra āsīt | nānyat kiñcana miṣat |
sa īkṣata lokān nu sṛjā iti ||1.1.1||
verses.english_translation: In the beginning, the Self alone existed here — nothing else whatsoever blinked. He thought: 'Let me create worlds.'
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: आत्मा — परमात्मा — एकः एव अग्रे आसीत्। सृष्टेः पूर्वम् आत्मा एव आसीत्। स ईक्षत — संकल्पं कृतवान् — लोकान् सृजामि इति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Before creation, the supreme Self alone existed. Nothing else — no world, no beings. The Self willed to create worlds. This shows that creation proceeds from the Self's own will, not from any external material.

verses.transliteration: prajñānaṃ brahma ||3.1.3||
verses.english_translation: Consciousness is Brahman.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: प्रज्ञानम् — विशुद्धं चैतन्यम् — ब्रह्म। इदम् महावाक्यम् — जीवब्रह्मैक्यं बोधयति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Pure consciousness is Brahman. This is one of the four great sayings (mahavakyas) of the Upanishads, from the Rigveda. It declares the identity of the individual consciousness with the supreme Brahman.

verses.sanskrit:
प्रज्ञेनैव पश्यति प्रज्ञेन शृणोति | प्रज्ञेन गन्धान् जिघ्रति प्रज्ञेन वाचं वदति |
प्रज्ञेन प्रियाप्रिये विजानाति ||३.१.२||
verses.transliteration:
prajñenaiva paśyati prajñena śṛṇoti | prajñena gandhān jighrati prajñena vācaṃ vadati |
prajñena priyāpriye vijānāti ||3.1.2||
verses.english_translation: By consciousness alone one sees, by consciousness one hears, by consciousness one smells fragrances, by consciousness one speaks, by consciousness one knows the pleasant and unpleasant.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: प्रज्ञेन — चैतन्येन — सर्वाणि इन्द्रियाणि प्रवर्तन्ते। चैतन्यम् एव सर्वस्य प्रवर्तकम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: All the senses function by consciousness alone. Consciousness is the activator of everything. Without consciousness, no sense organ can function. This leads to the conclusion: consciousness (prajna) is Brahman.

verses.sanskrit: स ईक्षत इमे नु लोका लोकपालान्नु सृजा इति | सोऽद्भ्य एव पुरुषमुद्धृत्य अमूर्च्छयत् ||१.१.३||
verses.transliteration: sa īkṣata ime nu lokā lokapālān nu sṛjā iti | so'dbhya eva puruṣam uddhṛtya amūrcchayat ||1.1.3||

word_meanings.meaning: let me create guardians of the worlds
drawing a person from the waters

verses.english_translation: He thought: 'These are the worlds. Let me create guardians of the worlds.' He drew a person from the waters and gave it form.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: लोकपालान् — लोकानां पालकान् — सृजामि इति ईक्षत। अद्भ्यः पुरुषम् उद्धृत्य — जलात् पुरुषाकारम् उद्धृत्य — अमूर्च्छयत् — मूर्तिमत् अकरोत्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Having created the worlds, the Self thought to create guardians for them. It drew a cosmic person from the primordial waters and gave it form — this is the creation of the cosmic body (virat).

verses.sanskrit: तं तपस्तप्त्वा मुखं निरभिद्यत यथाण्डं मुखाद्वाग्वाचोऽग्निः ||१.१.४||
verses.transliteration: taṃ tapas taptva mukhaṃ nirabhidyata yathāṇḍaṃ mukhād vāg vāco'gniḥ ||1.1.4||
verses.english_translation: Having brooded over it, the mouth burst open as an egg bursts. From the mouth came speech; from speech, fire.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: तपः तप्त्वा — ऊष्मणा — मुखम् निरभिद्यत। मुखात् वाक् — वाचः अग्निः — वाग्देवता अग्निः।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Through the heat of brooding (tapas), the cosmic body's organs burst open. From the mouth came speech, and the deity of speech is Agni (fire). This describes the cosmic origin of the organs of action.

verses.sanskrit: ता एता देवताः सृष्टा अस्मिन्महत्यर्णवे प्रापतन् | तमशनायापिपासाभ्यामन्ववार्जत् ||१.२.१||
verses.transliteration: tā etā devatāḥ sṛṣṭā asmin mahaty arṇave prāpatan | tam aśanāyāpipāsābhyām anvavārjat ||1.2.1||
verses.english_translation: These created deities fell into the great ocean. Hunger and thirst overcame him (the cosmic person).
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: देवताः — वाग्आदयः — अस्मिन् महति अर्णवे — संसारसागरे — प्रापतन्। अशनाया अपिपासाभ्याम् — क्षुत्पिपासाभ्याम् — अन्ववार्जत्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The created deities (speech, etc.) fell into the great ocean of samsara. The cosmic person was overcome by hunger and thirst — the fundamental drives that sustain embodied existence.

verses.sanskrit: सोऽकामयत | अन्नं मे सृज्यतामिति | स एतं रश्मिमभ्यतपत् | तस्माद्रश्मितप्तात् अन्नमजायत ||१.३.१||
verses.transliteration: so'kāmayata | annaṃ me sṛjyatām iti | sa etaṃ raśmim abhyatapat | tasmād raśmitaptāt annam ajāyata ||1.3.1||

word_meanings.meaning: let food be created for me

verses.english_translation: He desired: 'Let food be created for me.' He brooded over this ray. From that brooded-upon ray, food was born.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: अन्नम् — भोज्यम् — सृज्यताम् इति अकामयत। रश्मिम् अभ्यतपत् — सूर्यरश्मिम् ध्यात्वा — अन्नम् अजायत।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The Self desired food for the cosmic person. By brooding over the solar ray, food (the material world) was created. This describes the creation of the objective world as food for the subjective Self.

verses.sanskrit: स एतमेव सीमानं विदार्यैतया द्वारा प्रापद्यत | सैषा विदृतिर्नाम द्वास्तदेतन्नान्दनं नाम ||१.३.१२||
verses.transliteration: sa etam eva sīmānam vidāryaitayā dvārā prāpadyata | saiṣā vidṛtir nāma dvās tad etan nāndanaṃ nāma ||1.3.12||

word_meanings.meaning: splitting the crown of the head

verses.english_translation: Splitting the crown of the head, it entered by this door. This door is called Vidrti (the cleft). This is the place called Nandana (joy).
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: सीमानम् विदार्य — ब्रह्मरन्ध्रम् भित्त्वा — प्रविशत्। विदृतिः — ब्रह्मरन्ध्रम् — नान्दनम् — आनन्दस्थानम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The Self entered the cosmic body through the crown of the head (brahmarandhra). This is called Vidrti (the cleft) and Nandana (the place of joy) — because the Self's entry brings consciousness and bliss to the body.

verses.sanskrit: स जातो भूतान्यभिव्यैख्यत् | किमिहान्यं वावदिषदिति | स एतमेव पुरुषं ब्रह्म ततममपश्यत् | इदमदर्शमिति ||२.१.१||
verses.transliteration: sa jāto bhūtāny abhivyaikhyat | kim ihānyaṃ vāvadiṣad iti | sa etam eva puruṣaṃ brahma tatamam apaśyat | idam adarśam iti ||2.1.1||
verses.english_translation: Having been born, he looked upon all beings. What else would he have wished to speak? He saw this very Brahman spread everywhere. 'I have seen this,' he said.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: जातः — जन्म प्राप्य — भूतानि अभिव्यैख्यत् — सर्वाणि भूतानि अपश्यत्। ब्रह्म ततमम् — सर्वत्र व्याप्तम् ब्रह्म — अपश्यत्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: At birth, the wise one looked upon all beings and saw Brahman spread everywhere. 'I have seen this' — this is the vision of the knower who recognizes Brahman in all beings at the very moment of birth.

verses.sanskrit: प्रज्ञानेत्रश्च लोकः प्रज्ञाने प्रतिष्ठितः | प्रज्ञानेत्रं ब्रह्म | प्रज्ञानं ब्रह्म ||३.१.३||
verses.transliteration: prajñānetras ca lokaḥ prajñāne pratiṣṭhitaḥ | prajñānetraṃ brahma | prajñānaṃ brahma ||3.1.3||

word_meanings.meaning: the world has consciousness as its eye
Brahman has consciousness as its eye

verses.english_translation: The world has consciousness as its eye and is established in consciousness. Brahman has consciousness as its eye. Consciousness is Brahman.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: प्रज्ञानेत्रः लोकः — प्रज्ञानम् नेत्रम् यस्य — चैतन्यम् यस्य दृष्टिः। प्रज्ञानम् ब्रह्म — चैतन्यम् एव ब्रह्म।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The world has consciousness as its eye — it is seen and known through consciousness. Brahman itself has consciousness as its eye — Brahman is pure consciousness. Therefore: consciousness is Brahman. This is the mahavakya of the Rigveda.

verses.sanskrit: स ईक्षत इमे नु लोका लोकपालान्नु सृजा इति | सोऽद्भ्य एव पुरुषमुद्धृत्य अमूर्च्छयत् ||१.१.३||
verses.transliteration: sa īkṣata ime nu lokā lokapālān nu sṛjā iti | so'dbhya eva puruṣam uddhṛtya amūrcchayat ||1.1.3||

word_meanings.meaning: let me create guardians of the worlds
drawing a person from the waters

verses.english_translation: He thought: 'These are the worlds. Let me create guardians of the worlds.' He drew a person from the waters and gave it form.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: लोकपालान् — लोकानां पालकान् — सृजामि इति ईक्षत। अद्भ्यः पुरुषम् उद्धृत्य — जलात् पुरुषाकारम् उद्धृत्य — अमूर्च्छयत्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Having created the worlds, the Self thought to create guardians for them. It drew a cosmic person from the primordial waters and gave it form — this is the creation of the cosmic body (virat).

verses.sanskrit: नासिके प्राणमसृजत | तस्माद्वायुः | नेत्रे चक्षुरसृजत | तस्मादादित्यः ||१.१.४||
verses.transliteration: nāsike prāṇam asṛjat | tasmād vāyuḥ | netre cakṣur asṛjat | tasmād ādityaḥ ||1.1.4||

word_meanings.meaning: from the nostrils he created prana
from the eyes he created sight

verses.english_translation: From the nostrils he created prana — from that, Vayu. From the eyes he created sight — from that, the Sun.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: नासिकाभ्याम् प्राणः — वायुः। नेत्राभ्याम् चक्षुः — आदित्यः। प्रत्येकम् इन्द्रियात् देवता।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Each sense organ has a corresponding cosmic deity. From the nostrils/prana came Vayu (wind); from the eyes/sight came the Sun. This shows the correspondence between the microcosm (individual body) and the macrocosm (universe).

verses.sanskrit: तमशनायापिपासे अभ्यवर्तेताम् | सोऽकामयत | अन्नं मे सृज्यतामिति ||१.२.१||
verses.transliteration: tam aśanāyāpipāse abhyavartetām | so'kāmayata | annaṃ me sṛjyatām iti ||1.2.1||
verses.english_translation: Hunger and thirst approached him. He desired: 'Let food be created for me.'
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: अशनाया — क्षुत् — अपिपासा — तृष्णा — तम् अभ्यवर्तेताम्। सः अकामयत — अन्नम् सृज्यताम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The cosmic person experienced hunger and thirst — the fundamental drives of embodied existence. He desired food to be created. This describes the creation of the objective world as the food for the subjective Self.

verses.sanskrit: तान्येतानि कर्माणि सत्यानि | अथ खल्वेतानि कर्माणि सत्यानि | अथ खल्वेतानि कर्माणि सत्यानि ||२.१.१||
verses.transliteration: tāny etāni karmāṇi satyāni | atha khalv etāni karmāṇi satyāni | atha khalv etāni karmāṇi satyāni ||2.1.1||
verses.english_translation: These actions are indeed real. Now indeed these actions are real. Now indeed these actions are real.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: एतानि कर्माणि — सृष्टिकर्माणि — सत्यानि — वास्तवानि। त्रिवारम् उक्तम् — दृढीकरणाय।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The acts of creation are real — not illusory. The threefold repetition emphasizes the reality of the creative process. This prepares for the teaching about the three births of the Self.

verses.sanskrit: गर्भो ह वा अयमस्मिन् रेतः सिच्यते | तदेतत्सर्वाङ्गेभ्यस्तेजः समभरत् | तन्नारीमेव गच्छति ||२.१.३||
verses.transliteration: garbho ha vā ayam asmin retaḥ sicyate | tad etat sarvāṅgebhyas tejaḥ samabharat | tan nārīm eva gacchati ||2.1.3||
verses.english_translation: This is the first birth: the seed is sown in him. That vitality gathered from all limbs goes to the woman.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: प्रथमम् जन्म — गर्भाधानम्। रेतः — बीजम् — नारीम् गच्छति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The first birth is conception — when the seed (carrying the Self) is sown. The vitality gathered from all limbs of the father goes to the mother. This is the beginning of the Self's journey into a new body.

verses.sanskrit: स जायमानो यं लोकं जायते तेन सम्भवति | स एव तस्य द्वितीयं जन्म ||२.१.४||
verses.transliteration: sa jāyamāno yaṃ lokaṃ jāyate tena sambhavati | sa eva tasya dvitīyaṃ janma ||2.1.4||

word_meanings.meaning: the world into which he is born
he comes into being through it

verses.english_translation: Being born, he comes into being through the world into which he is born. That is his second birth.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: द्वितीयम् जन्म — मातुः गर्भात् जन्म। यम् लोकम् जायते — यस्मिन् लोके जन्म प्राप्नोति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The second birth is physical birth from the mother's womb. The Self comes into the world and takes on a new identity. This is the birth that most people consider the only birth.

verses.sanskrit: तस्य प्रेतस्य यं लोकं प्रेष्यन्ति | तस्मात्सम्भवति | तत्तृतीयं जन्म ||२.१.५||
verses.transliteration: tasya pretasya yaṃ lokaṃ preṣyanti | tasmāt sambhavati | tat tṛtīyaṃ janma ||2.1.5||

word_meanings.meaning: the world to which they send him

verses.english_translation: Of the departed, the world to which they send him — from that he comes into being. That is the third birth.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: तृतीयम् जन्म — मृत्योः परम् — परलोके जन्म। प्रेतस्य — मृतस्य — यम् लोकम् प्रेष्यन्ति — तस्मात् सम्भवति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The third birth is after death — the rebirth in another world or another body. The Aitareya teaches that the Self undergoes three births: conception, physical birth, and rebirth after death. Liberation means transcending all three births.

verses.sanskrit: तदुक्तमृषिणा | गर्भे नु सन्नन्वेषामवेदमहं देवानां जनिमानि विश्वा | शतं मा पुर आयसीररक्षन्नधः श्येनो जवसा निरदीयम् ||२.१.५||
verses.transliteration: tad uktam ṛṣiṇā | garbhe nu sann anv eṣām avedam ahaṃ devānāṃ janimāni viśvā | śataṃ mā pura āyasīr arakṣann adhaḥ śyeno javasā niradīyam ||2.1.5||
verses.english_translation: This was said by the seer: 'While still in the womb, I knew all the births of the gods. A hundred iron fortresses held me; then like a hawk with speed I flew down.'
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: वामदेवः — गर्भे एव — ज्ञानम् प्राप्तवान्। शतम् पुराः — शरीरम् — आयसीः — दृढाः। श्येनः इव — निरदीयम् — मुक्तः अभवत्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The sage Vamadeva attained knowledge even in the womb. The 'hundred iron fortresses' represent the body and its limitations. Like a hawk breaking free, he attained liberation. This shows that knowledge of the Self can be attained at any stage of life.

verses.sanskrit: प्रज्ञेनैव पश्यति प्रज्ञेन शृणोति | प्रज्ञेन गन्धान् जिघ्रति प्रज्ञेन वाचं वदति ||३.१.२||
verses.transliteration: prajñenaiva paśyati prajñena śṛṇoti | prajñena gandhān jighrati prajñena vācaṃ vadati ||3.1.2||
verses.english_translation: By consciousness alone one sees, by consciousness one hears, by consciousness one smells fragrances, by consciousness one speaks.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: प्रज्ञेन — चैतन्येन — सर्वाणि इन्द्रियाणि प्रवर्तन्ते। चैतन्यम् एव सर्वस्य प्रवर्तकम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: All the senses function by consciousness alone. Consciousness is the activator of everything. Without consciousness, no sense organ can function. This leads to the conclusion: consciousness (prajna) is Brahman.

verses.sanskrit: हृदयं वा इदं ब्रह्म | एतद्ब्रह्म | एतत्सर्वम् ||३.१.३||
verses.transliteration: hṛdayaṃ vā idaṃ brahma | etad brahma | etat sarvam ||3.1.3||
verses.english_translation: This heart is indeed Brahman. This is Brahman. This is all.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: हृदयम् — चित्तम् — ब्रह्म। एतत् सर्वम् — सर्वम् ब्रह्मैव।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The heart (hridaya) — the seat of consciousness — is Brahman. This is Brahman. This is all. The Aitareya concludes with the simplest and most direct statement: everything is Brahman, and Brahman is consciousness.
